data.content:
TBAD
Type B Aortic Dissection (TBAD) is a specific subtype of aortic dissection involving a tear in the aorta, accounting for 33% of all aortic dissection types according to IRAD. Its causes and risk factors include hypertension, atherosclerosis, genetic mutations, trauma, and anatomical variations. The condition can lead to severe complications such as ischemia, heart issues, neurological deficits, and rupture. Currently, TBAD lacks specific clinical biomarkers, necessitating comprehensive imaging for preoperative planning.

TBAD is categorized into acute, subacute, and chronic phases, with pathology and patient condition dictating management strategies. It requires further classification based on clinical presentation and imaging features, generally dividing into uncomplicated or high-risk categories. More specifically, acute TBAD is categorized into acute complex, acute high-risk, and acute non-complex types, each with specific intervention timings and management strategies. TBAD is treated with Thoracic Endovascular Aortic Repair (TEVAR); however, chronic TBAD carries a higher incidence of distal stent graft-induced new entry (SINE) compared to the acute phase. Postoperative care requires strict blood pressure management, and the condition carries an ongoing risk of reintervention.
